video_title: Taiwan’s War on Renewables: The Political Economy of Energy Poverty
gist: Taiwan imports ~97% of its energy and has spent 40 years dismantling nuclear while botching a renewables rollout, leaving it structurally addicted to seaborne LNG and acutely vulnerable to a Chinese blockade; artificially cheap, state-monopoly electricity pricing, corruption, and failed local-content mandates have starved the grid just as TSMC and AI data-center demand surges, though President Lai's March 2026 nuclear-restart reversal signals a possible turn.

insights.claim: Taiwan imports roughly 97% of its energy, the highest rate of any major economy, and holds only about 11 days of LNG reserves and roughly 42 days of coal, meaning a sustained naval blockade would cut power within weeks.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Taiwan's grid is a single point of failure that China can attack without firing a shot, making energy the softest target in any coercion scenario.
insights.quote: 97% of Taiwan's energy comes from imported sources, the highest rate of any major economy.

insights.claim: CSIS wargaming found that once Taiwan's coal reserves (roughly 40 days combined) are exhausted under blockade, total electricity production would fall to about 20% of pre-blockade levels, at which point all manufacturing ceases.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: A blockade need not sink ships to halt Taiwan's chip output; strangling fuel imports collapses the fab economy within ~40 days.
insights.quote: wargaming by CSIS found that Taiwan's total electricity production would fall to about 20% of pre-blockade levels, at which point all manufacturing would cease.

insights.claim: China could disrupt Taiwan's gas supply below the threshold of an act of war by using its coast guard/maritime vessels to impose fabricated permit requirements on energy tankers, rather than launching a formal blockade or firing missiles.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Gray-zone energy coercion is a more likely and deniable path than kinetic blockade, so Taiwan's LNG-heavy grid is exposed even short of war.
insights.quote: they have the means to say oh we have new renewable energy mandate. So any energy vessels coming into our territorial waters have to get this permit and if you don't have a permit then you're out of line.

insights.claim: Cutting Taiwan's gas supply would require blockading only three LNG import terminals, whereas distributed renewables are far harder to disable because killing offshore wind means severing undersea cables in the ADIZ and killing rooftop solar would require bombing apartment buildings.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Distributed renewables are a wartime resilience asset, reframing the energy debate as a defense issue, not just a climate one.
insights.quote: To cut Taiwan's gas supply, China would only need to blockade three LG terminals.

insights.claim: Taiwan is currently avoiding an LNG price crisis during the 2026 Iran war and Hormuz closure only because Chinese natural-gas demand destruction and idle coal capacity freed up excess spot cargoes, an ironic dependence on Beijing for supply.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Taiwan's spot-market cushion is a lucky byproduct of Chinese economic conditions, not a durable hedge, and could vanish if Chinese demand recovers.
insights.quote: I think Taiwan should send a thank you note and a fruit basket to Beijing right now because the reason why we are not having an energy crisis right now is because of China.

insights.claim: Taiwan gets roughly a third of its LNG from Qatar and was contracted for 35% from that source, so the Iran war and damage to Qatar's largest LNG complex forced it onto a spot market where Asian gas prices surged over 140%.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Concentration of LNG sourcing in a single Gulf supplier magnifies Taiwan's exposure to Middle East chokepoint risk on top of China risk.
insights.quote: Taiwan, which gets roughly a third of its LG fromQatar, is paying the price.

themes.theme: Tai Power Monopoly & Distorted Electricity Pricing

insights.claim: Tai Power controls 100% of Taiwan's fossil-fuel and nuclear generation plus all transmission, distribution, and retail, owning everything except renewables, so government fuel bailouts overwhelmingly subsidize fossil generation over clean energy.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: The monopoly structure hardwires a fossil-fuel bias into subsidies, structurally disadvantaging every merchant renewable and storage developer.
insights.quote: the only thing they don't own is renewables... So these bailouts are overwhelmingly subsidizing fossil fuels.

insights.claim: By mid-2025 Tai Power had accumulated 418 billion NTD (about 13 billion USD) in losses because household electricity prices were frozen at 3.78 NTD/kWh (~12 cents US) even as LNG prices soared 150% after the Iran war.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: A state utility bleeding taxpayer money to suppress prices signals no near-term path to market liberalization or profitable merchant power.
insights.quote: By mid 2025, Tai Power had accumulated 418 billion NTD in losses, which is about 13 billion USD.

insights.claim: Taiwan keeps electricity prices artificially low primarily as monetary policy, because electricity is a key CPI input and holding CPI below 2% removes pressure to raise interest rates.
insights.speaker: Jason Fun
insights.so_what: Energy pricing is hostage to macro policy, so market-based reform faces entrenched central-bank/political resistance, not just utility inertia.
insights.quote: The reason the government wants the electricity price to be kept so is monetary policy. Because electricity price is one of the important factors in CPI.

insights.claim: Artificially low conventional prices (averaging ~3 NTD/kWh) versus renewable prices of 4.5 to 5 NTD/kWh ensure renewables cannot compete on cost, so absent mandates or subsidies consumers default to fossil power.
insights.speaker: Tang Lu
insights.so_what: Suppressed fossil prices are the binding barrier to voluntary renewable uptake, meaning decarbonization stalls without price reform.
insights.quote: renewable energy prices like 4.5 to 5 new Taiwan per kilowatt hour compared to the conventional price averaging three new Taiwan.

insights.claim: On May 9th, 2025, Taiwan's legislature formally rolled back the 2016 requirement to unbundle Tai Power, legislation sponsored by DPP lawmakers and passed with cross-party support and cheered by Tai Power's union.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: The reversal entrenches the monopoly indefinitely, closing off the market structure needed for merchant renewables and storage arbitrage.
insights.quote: On May 9th, 2025, Taiwan's legislative UN formally rolled back the requirement to break up Tai Power.

insights.claim: Tai Power discriminates by industry when raising prices, immediately hitting high-margin sectors like semiconductors and AI data centers while granting lower-margin industries a delay, which discourages efficiency innovation and slows economic development.
insights.speaker: Jason Fun
insights.so_what: Selective pricing burdens Taiwan's most productive export sectors and blunts price signals that would otherwise drive efficiency investment.
insights.quote: These are the ones that make more money. They will be directly impacted by this price increase... you can enjoy a delay of that electricity price increase.

insights.claim: A think-tank view holds that unbundling Tai Power is not the binding constraint; the real hurdles are limited land, administrative bottlenecks, and grid infrastructure, and the renewable market is already largely driven by independent power producers.
insights.speaker: Tang Lu
insights.so_what: Even fixing the monopoly would not unlock renewables if land, permitting, and grid limits remain, tempering the case for structural reform alone.
insights.quote: unbundling uh TPC is not a prerequisite to solving our current development because the primary hurdles as I mentioned is about limited land mass administrative bottlenecks and green infrastructure.

insights.claim: Taiwan's Round 3.1 and 3.2 offshore wind auctions awarded points for local-content commitments even though Taiwan has no wind-turbine supply chain, so made-in-Taiwan components cost two to three times imported parts and destroyed project profitability.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Localization mandates without domestic scale are a template for how industrial policy can inflate costs and kill the very projects they aim to build.
insights.quote: made in Taiwan components ended up costing sometimes two three times the components um made outside of Taiwan dragged the whole uh profitability picture down.

insights.claim: An EU challenge at the WTO filed in July 2024, settled by Taiwan in November 2024, forced Round 3.3 to drop localization requirements entirely, stranding developers who won 3.1/3.2 under now-impossible commitments the government admits cannot be met.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Policy whiplash traps earlier winners in unfulfillable contracts while later rounds get better terms, poisoning developer trust and freezing buildout.
insights.quote: round 3.3 would drop localization requirements entirely, prompted by an EU challenge at the World Trade Organization filed in July 2024.

insights.claim: Major international developers exited Taiwan: RWE announced its withdrawal in November 2023 and JERA sold its Formosa 3 stake to Corio and TotalEnergies in 2023, leaving Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners as a key remaining developer still progressing on 3.1/3.2 but with no turbines yet generating power.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Jason Fun
insights.so_what: The exodus of blue-chip developers signals to capital that Taiwan offshore wind carries policy and execution risk few will underwrite.
insights.quote: The German utility RWE announced in November 2023 that it was pulling out of Taiwan. Jera, the Japanese giant, sold its stake in Formosa 3 to Coro and Total Energies in 2023.

insights.claim: The flagship 640 MW Yunlin wind farm reached full operation only in early 2025 after years of delays, and its 70 billion NTD financing (signed 2019 with 19 banks) fell into emergency restructuring, forcing sponsors to inject roughly three times their original equity.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Triple equity overruns on the flagship project quantify how badly Taiwan wind economics broke, warning off future project finance.
insights.quote: saw its 70 billion new Taiwan dollar financing deal, originally signed in 2019 with 19 banks, fall into emergency restructuring. sponsors had to put in roughly three times their original equity commitments

insights.claim: By late 2024 Taiwan had installed only 2.8 GW of offshore wind against an original 5.7 GW target, as localization costs compounded post-Ukraine commodity spikes and rising interest rates that broke wind projects globally.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Angelica
insights.so_what: The ~50% shortfall against target shows how far behind schedule Taiwan's flagship renewable is, deepening reliance on imported gas.
insights.quote: by late 2024, Taiwan had installed just 2.8 8 g of offshore wind, far below its original 5.7 gawatt target.

insights.claim: Taiwan blocked Chinese firms from its offshore wind industry but then favored made-in-Taiwan components over parts from democratic partners like Europe and Japan, forgoing a chance to give allied developers scale against Chinese overcapacity.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Protectionism aimed at building a local supply chain squandered a strategic opening to deepen allied clean-energy supply chains excluding China.
insights.quote: the DPP government privileged made in Taiwan components over parts made by democratic partners, and these projects have been phenomenally expensive.

insights.claim: Offshore wind advocates argue the shortfall reflects rapid demand growth outpacing infrastructure, not technology failure, noting renewables' share tripled from 4.2% (2015) to 12.7% and 2024 actual generation of 288.9 billion kWh exceeded the 257.5 billion kWh projection by 31.4 billion kWh.
insights.speaker: Tang Lu
insights.so_what: If demand growth, not tech, is the constraint, Taiwan's renewable buildout may be less of a dead end than headlines suggest, but the gap keeps widening.
insights.quote: there is an increase of 31.4 billion kilows over the original estimates equivalent uh to the annual operator of actually 4.5 new gas fire units which is really big.

insights.claim: Solar developers split large projects into smaller ones to dodge a 2020 ban on converting small farmland to solar; the Lyang Energy case indicted 15 people including its chairman and an Energy Bureau solar director over forged documents, with estimated illegal profits of 9.1 billion NTD (~300 million USD).
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Systemic circumvention of land rules has fused renewables with corruption in public perception, poisoning the political license to build.
insights.quote: The estimated illegal profits ran to 9.1 billion New Taiwan dollars, close to 300 million US. And that's just one company.

insights.claim: Local officials extract bribes for solar permits: Kohou Township's former mayor Lin Huling demanded a bribe from Poseidon Solar (negotiated from 8 million to 4 million NTD, ~125,000 USD) to expedite permits and eliminate protests; permits cleared in two weeks, he got 10 years, and the project became a deserted wasteland.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Local extortion (the 'green energy cockroaches') is a direct tax on renewable developers and abandons scarce sited land.
insights.quote: The original ask was an $8 million new Taiwan bribe negotiated down to $4 million new Taiwan... in exchange for expediting permits and quote eliminating public protests.

insights.claim: Chung Yao-lin, Tsai Ing-wen's young point-person for offshore wind (dubbed 'little Ing-wen' and Tsai's 'renewable whisperer'), was detained August 2025 and indicted December 26, 2025 on bribery/money laundering charges over 1.98 million NTD in bribes and 6 million NTD in unexplained assets, with prosecutors seeking 14 years.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Prosecution of the wind program's architect signals both entrenched corruption and a political purge that chills further development.
insights.quote: He was basically the government's point person for the entire offshore wind industry under Tying Wen... Prosecutors are seeking 14 years.

insights.claim: A source argues President Lai's anti-corruption 'war on green cockroaches' is really an intra-DPP purge targeting officials tied to predecessor Tsai Ing-wen rather than reforming the permitting system, with the renewable industry as collateral damage rooted in the 2019 DPP primary rivalry.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: If crackdowns are factional rather than systemic, corruption incentives persist and the renewable industry keeps suffering reputational and political damage.
insights.quote: President lie has declared war on the green cockroaches but critics note that he's mainly targeting officials associated with his predecessor Tyanguen not reforming the permitting system.

insights.claim: Rooftop solar permitting in Taiwan averages over a year, worse than California's ~six months and far worse than Australia's ~one-to-six weeks, and officials deliberately slow-walk approvals for months out of fear of corruption-related repercussions rather than genuine technical review.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: Permitting friction, worsened by anti-renewable optics, is a self-reinforcing bottleneck that no amount of subsidy fixes without process standardization.
insights.quote: in Taiwan. If you want to do rooftop solar, it is well over a year. It is on average a year or maybe even over a year. So, it is even worse in California.

insights.claim: Renewable corruption is often driven by relatively unseen mid-level bureaucrats rather than high officials, and anti-renewable interest groups (nimbys, nuclear or gas advocates) have weaponized and exaggerated corruption narratives to attack the sector.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: The corruption story is partly real and partly a political weapon, so investors should discount sensationalist accounts but not dismiss governance risk.
insights.quote: Often times it's the midlevel you know like bureaucrat that no one knows about that kind of um is the mastermind behind all of this

insights.claim: Taiwan built a meaningful battery fleet but for the wrong market: because only Tai Power could sell conventional electricity and batteries couldn't prove their stored energy was purely renewable, storage operators were locked out of energy arbitrage and confined to frequency regulation, ancillary services, and reserve capacity.
insights.speaker: Jason Fun / Narrator
insights.so_what: The monopoly's rules foreclosed the primary revenue model for grid batteries, misallocating capital into markets too small to sustain.
insights.quote: When the battery energy storage system cannot prove that all the energy they store is from renewables, they are not allowed to do power trading in Taiwan.

insights.claim: As too many battery operators piled into Taiwan's small, fixed ancillary-services and reserve markets, auction prices collapsed to zero, so essentially all Taiwanese batteries now suffer from zero-price auctions.
insights.speaker: Jason Fun
insights.so_what: Zero clearing prices mean stranded storage assets and no viable business case for merchant batteries under the current market design.
insights.quote: When the supply exceeds the demand, the auction price will drop to zero. All the batteries in Taiwan now suffer from this zero price thing.

insights.claim: Arbitrage is a non-starter because Tai Power charges the same price at peak and overnight for 98% of households and only 6-7 NTD/kWh more at peak for industrial users, so without hourly price swings batteries cannot profit and non-intermittent gas cannot be replaced by renewables.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Flat pricing structurally blocks storage-enabled renewables integration, locking in gas dependence and perpetual Tai Power bailouts.
insights.quote: Tai Power charges the same price for electricity consumed at peak times versus in the middle of the night for 98% of households, which makes arbitrage a non-starter.

insights.claim: The only battery economics that work in Taiwan are behind-the-meter storage near industrial customers like semiconductor fabs, and in March 2026 the Ministry of Economic Affairs announced a subsidy up to 5 million NTD per MWh for industrial behind-the-meter storage, with Chinese-made batteries excluded.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Policymakers are subsidizing private bill-management rather than building a merchant storage market, signaling where non-Chinese battery vendors can find demand.
insights.quote: the Ministry of Economic Affairs announced a subsidy of up to 5 million NTD per megawatt hour for industrial users installing behind the meter storage.

themes.theme: Grid Constraints on TSMC & AI Data Centers

insights.claim: Only about 16% of Taiwan's electricity comes from domestic renewable or nuclear power while TSMC alone consumes 8-10% of the grid, leaving ~84% dependent on seaborne fossil imports.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger) / Angelica
insights.so_what: TSMC's outsized single-company power draw against a mostly imported grid concentrates both economic and geopolitical risk in one point of failure.
insights.quote: Only about 16% of Taiwan's electricity comes from domestic renewable or nuclear power. And TSMC alone consumes 8 to 10% of Taiwan's electricity.

insights.claim: Taiwan's grid is so tight that you cannot site even a 5 MW data center in northern Taiwan (the industrial heartland), and an 80 MW project in the south had to be split into four ~20 MW phases across different townships due to power shortages.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Power scarcity is actively capping Taiwan's ability to capture hyperscaler data-center investment, leaving money on the table for competitors in Japan/US.
insights.quote: the fact that you can't even site a five megawatt data center in North Taiwan, which is the industrial heartland, is pathetic.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers like Google and Amazon would build extensive data centers in Taiwan given its central, ideal conditions but are constrained purely by electricity, and the chip sector itself is similarly power-constrained.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Electricity, not talent or geography, is now the gating factor for Taiwan's AI/compute economy, making grid buildout an economic-growth imperative.
insights.quote: Google, Amazon, all these tech giants, they would love to build infinite data centers in Taiwan if they could... but they are constrained by electricity.

insights.claim: Taiwan has no market or pricing mechanism to curb electricity demand growth, so when chip manufacturers demand more power the government's reflex is always to add supply rather than deploy efficiency or demand-response, leaving low-hanging efficiency gains untapped because prices are kept dirt cheap.
insights.speaker: Yu-Su
insights.so_what: Demand-side reform (peak pricing, efficiency) may be a more feasible lever than supply expansion, but flat cheap prices remove any incentive to pursue it.
insights.quote: every time there's an expansion need from the especially the chip manufacturing industry... the government's first respond is to come up with more but they really don't have this way to use any for example efficiency measure.

insights.claim: On March 22, 2026, President Lai announced that decommissioned plants 2 (Guosheng) and 3 (Maanshan) meet conditions to restart, citing AI-driven demand and decarbonization, with Maanshan potentially restarting by end of 2028, a stunning reversal less than a year after Taiwan achieved its 'non-nuclear homeland' goal.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: A DPP president reversing 40 years of anti-nuclear branding marks a structural policy shift that could add base-load supply and reshape the energy debate.
insights.quote: Lie publicly announced that two decommissioned nuclear plants, plant number two, Guang and plant number three, Maans, meet conditions to restart.

insights.claim: Restarting the two reactors would cover only about 10% of Taiwan's current electricity needs, but a single reactor's ~6% already exceeds every solar panel on the island combined, and completing the never-finished fourth plant could raise nuclear's share to 16-18%.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Nuclear offers concentrated, dispatchable output that dwarfs current solar, but two restarts alone are useful rather than transformative for the supply gap.
insights.quote: the 6% from one reactor is already more electricity than every solar panel on the island combined.

insights.claim: The never-completed Longmen (fourth) plant is an advanced boiling water reactor designed by GE with reactor units from Hitachi and Toshiba, turbines from Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, and construction by Shimizu, but was mothballed in 2014 and permanently blocked by a 2021 referendum after opponents smeared it as a dangerous multi-supplier 'Franken reactor.'
insights.speaker: Narrator / Angelica
insights.so_what: A finished large ABWR asset sits idle; reviving it is technically the biggest lever but politically the hardest given the entrenched smear narrative.
insights.quote: Long was supposed to be the next generation reactor, an advanced boiling water reactor designed by GE with Hitachi and Toshiba building the actual reactor units.

insights.claim: By unilaterally reversing the nuclear shutdown, President Lai paradoxically removed his own negotiating leverage, since the opposition now knows he actually favors nuclear, so pro-nuclear carrots are no longer enough to trade for renewable reforms in a nuclear-for-renewables quid pro quo.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: The political-economy deadlock means an 'all of the above' grand bargain remains unlikely, so reforms will stay incremental and crisis-driven.
insights.quote: by reversing the nuclear shutdown unilaterally, lie has paradoxically removed his own leverage.

insights.claim: Taiwan's energy debate posits nuclear and renewables as mutually exclusive rival teams (DPP pro-renewables/anti-nuclear; KMT and TPP pro-nuclear but increasingly anti-renewables), so climate pressure fails to drive cross-party cooperation and the 'all of the above' constituency is unrepresented in the legislature.
insights.speaker: Yu-Su / Narrator
insights.so_what: Polarization structurally blocks the pragmatic energy mix Taiwan needs, so gridlock, not technology, is the core investment risk.
insights.quote: nuclear and renewables are on opposite teams and posited as mutually exclusive energy paths instead of compliments.

insights.claim: In February 2026 Taiwan signed a reciprocal trade agreement with the Trump administration committing to over 40 billion USD in US LNG and crude oil purchases through 2029, which will raise US LNG to roughly one-third of Taiwan's total supply.
insights.speaker: Narrator (Lily Ottinger)
insights.so_what: Taiwan is diversifying LNG toward the US for geopolitical alignment, creating a durable multi-billion-dollar US energy export flow but deepening overall gas dependence.
insights.quote: Taiwan signed a reciprocal trade agreement with the Trump administration, committing to over $40 billion in US LG and crude oil purchases through 2029.

insights.claim: The US government's priority in energy cooperation with Taiwan is selling LNG and oil rather than transferring clean-energy technology, though Taiwan could benefit from applying fracking-derived techniques to geothermal as companies like Fervo are doing.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: Expect US-Taiwan energy ties to center on fossil exports; geothermal/tech collaboration is a secondary, uncertain opportunity for allied firms.
insights.quote: the primary priority of the US government or the current administration is to sell Taiwan more LG or more you know like oil.

insights.claim: A proposed 'Pax Electricana' would offshore Taiwan's most energy-intensive heavy industry, having partners like Australia perform the iron-making step (which consumes ~85% of steelmaking energy) using cheap clean power before shipping semi-processed iron to Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: Energy-arbitrage offshoring of heavy industry is a speculative structural idea, but carries obvious risk of moving strategic supply offshore amid invasion threats.
insights.quote: The iron making step actually accounts for uh 85% of the energy consumption of the entire process.

insights.claim: Taiwan's energy failure risks a major electoral backlash against the DPP in this year's local elections and the 2028 presidential race driven by competence frustration rather than cross-strait sentiment, potentially reviving the pro-Beijing-leaning KMT despite public dislike of its China ties.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Energy poverty is a live domestic political risk that could shift Taiwan's government and, by extension, its cross-strait posture, a geopolitical wildcard for investors.
insights.quote: there's a level of sick and tired with the DPP in Taiwan that's got nothing to do with crossstrait relations. It's got to do with their incompetence.

insights.claim: Taiwan ranks second globally (after the Netherlands) in rooftop solar density on flat land despite solar being under 10% of its electricity mix, meaning much of the easiest solar potential has already been exhausted.
insights.speaker: Ricky Huang
insights.so_what: High existing rooftop density means future solar growth will be harder and costlier, capping how much more distributed solar can offset import dependence.
insights.quote: Taiwan has higher rooftop solar density than any country we typically associate with high solar capacity, but it's actually number two globally after the Netherlands.

insights.claim: The core lesson framed for the West is that Taiwan's renewable failure is a case study in the ugly side of industrial policy, where overpromising aggressive targets combined with excessive money relative to project pipeline made corruption an inevitable product.
insights.speaker: Angelica
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers should read Taiwan as a cautionary template: aggressive green targets plus local-content mandates plus subsidy floods breed cost overruns and graft.
insights.quote: when the amount of money is excessive to the amount of pipeline and bandwidth, um, corruption is just the inevitable product of that scenario.
